questionText: A student writes: 'If two lines in a plane are parallel then they never meet.' Write down the converse of this statement, and discuss whether the converse is true, working throughout within a single plane. Hence state which symbol ($\Rightarrow$, $\Leftarrow$ or $\Leftrightarrow$) correctly connects the two statements.

steps.description: Identify the hypothesis and conclusion of the original statement.
steps.workingLatex: P:\ \text{the lines are parallel}, \qquad Q:\ \text{the lines never meet}
steps.explanation: An 'if... then...' statement has the form $P \Rightarrow Q$. Naming the two halves separately lets us handle the converse cleanly.

steps.description: Recall how the converse is formed.
steps.explanation: The converse simply swaps the hypothesis and conclusion, reversing the direction of the arrow. It is a genuinely different claim, so its truth must be checked on its own.

steps.workingLatex: \text{'If two lines in a plane never meet, then they are parallel.'}
steps.explanation: Applying the swap to the original sentence gives the converse. This now claims that non-meeting is enough to conclude the lines are parallel.

steps.description: Confirm the original statement is true.
steps.workingLatex: P \Rightarrow Q:\ \text{parallel lines keep a constant gap, so never meet}
steps.explanation: Parallel lines stay the same distance apart, so they can never cross. The original implication is therefore sound.

steps.description: Test the converse using how two coplanar lines can relate.
steps.workingLatex: \text{Two lines in one plane: either they intersect, or they are parallel}
steps.explanation: Within a single plane there are only two possibilities for a pair of distinct lines. This clean dichotomy is what makes the converse work here.

steps.description: Deduce the converse from the dichotomy.
steps.explanation: If two coplanar lines never meet, the 'intersecting' option is ruled out, leaving only 'parallel'. So in a plane, never meeting does force the lines to be parallel.

steps.description: Note why the single-plane restriction matters.
steps.workingLatex: \text{In 3D: skew lines never meet yet are not parallel}
steps.explanation: The dichotomy fails in three dimensions, where skew lines never meet but are not parallel. Restricting to one plane is exactly what keeps the converse true.

steps.description: Combine both directions into the connecting symbol.
steps.workingLatex: P \Rightarrow Q \ \text{and}\ Q \Rightarrow P \;\Longrightarrow\; P \Leftrightarrow Q
steps.explanation: Both the original and its converse are true for coplanar lines, so each statement implies the other and the correct connective is the two-way arrow.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Converse: 'If two lines in a plane never meet, then they are parallel.' Within a single plane this converse is TRUE, so the statements are equivalent: $P \Leftrightarrow Q$. (In 3D the converse would fail because of skew lines.)
